If Helios or the sun was conceived as driving from East to West, nothing seemed more natural than to assign to Eos also two horses, and to call them Lampros and Phaeton. When chariots were drawn by four horses in Greece, Eos also received four instead of two steeds 1.

Her epithets require hardly any commentary. Aly\rj(r<ra is the brilliant ; x.apo-mj is the joyful-eyed, the Sanskrit haryaksha ; xPv<T^0povos is the dawn sitting on a golden throne ; TJpiyeWa'is the early-born ; \vk6tto>\os is Eos drawn by white horses ; kevKoirrcpos. Eos with white wings ; $aeo-i/x/3poros, Eos who brings light to mortals. The rest is added by poets who speak of her as pobobdicrvkos, rosy-fingered; KpoKoireirkos, clothed in yellow garments ; ewrAoKa/xos, with beautiful ringlets ; x^o/3Ae'(£apoj, with eyelids white as snow. Latin poets add new epithets, such as lutea, rosy; pallida, pale; purpurea, purple-coloured: roscida, dewy ; vigil, wakeful.

You can see from these epithets, which gathered round the name of Eos in Greek, and Aurora in Latin, how inevitably what we call mythology springs up from the soil of language. As soon as a name, such as Eos, was thrown out, it grew and gathered new materials round itself, and without any special intention or effort became what we call a myth. Even such simple sentences as ' Eos is born,' 'Eos brings light,' ' Eos dies or disappears,' are changed at once into myth, fable, and legend, and it seems impossible to draw a line between what is simple language and what is myth.

MYTHOLOGY. 417 Mythology universal.

It was long supposed that much of what we call mythology was due to the peculiar poetical genius of the Greeks. Our first acquaintance with mythology came from Greece, and we were accustomed from our school-days to look upon the Greeks as a nation endowed with such wonderful gifts that we thought we might safely credit them with the invention of all the beauty and wisdom of their mythology. That there were dark sides to that bright picture also, could not be denied ; but it was thought possible by classical scholars, unacquainted with the mythology of other nations, that all that was hideous and foolish in classical mythology might be explained as a survival of barbarous ages, when the barbarous ancestors of Greeks and Romans were not above committing themselves those crimes and follies which they fondly ascribed to their gods.
It is here that Comparative Mythology has stepped in, and helped us to solve many difficulties which could not be removed by any other theory.
What is Comparative Mythology ?
Comparative Mythology and its three divisions.

Comparative is a name which has been assumed of late by nearly all historical and natural sciences, though, if we once understand the true method and purpose of any single science, it would seem to be almost superfluous to qualify it by that predicate. There is no science of single things, and all progress in human knowledge is achieved through comparison, leading on to the discovery of what different objects share in common, till we reach the widest generalisa- E e 418 LECTURE XVI.
tions and the highest ideas that are within the ken of human knowledge.
Comparative Philology.

Thus with regard to languages, the very first steps in our knowledge of words are made by comparison. What does grammar consist in but a collection of words which, though they differ from each other, share certain formal elements in common'? These formal elements are called grammatical elements, or suffixes, affixes, prefixes, etc., and we are said to know the grammar of a language when we have learnt under what conditions different words undergo the same formal modifications.
Thus comparison leads in the first instance to a grammatical knowledge of a single language.

When, however, we proceed from a study of one to a study of many languages, a new process of comparison begins. We observe that words in different languages undergo the same or nearly the same modifications, and by placing the paradigms of their declension and conjugation side by side, we try to find out on what points they agree and on what points they differ, and we hope thus to discover in the end the reasons why they should agree on certain points, and why they should differ on others.

Comparative Philology deals partly with facts, that is, the differences and coincidences that can be observed in the material and formal elements of language ; partly with laws, — using that word in the humble sense of ' something which is true of many objects,' not, as some scholars imagine, in that of v6[xol vtyCirobes ovpavtav be alOtpa Tkvu)0VT'S, &v "OXv^ttos MYTHOLOQY. 419 TTCLTllp fJLOl'OS, Ovbt VIV OvaTCL (f)V(TLS CLVtpiOV £TLKTV. These laws are to account for such peculiarities as give to each language its own distinctive character.

This science of Comparative Philology, however, very soon assumed three different aspects, and was cultivated in three schools, which may be called (1) the Etymological, or genealogical, (2) the Analogical, and (3) the Psychological.
Etymological School.

In comparing such languages as Latin, Greek, and Sanskrit, it was soon found that they were really varieties only of one and the same historical prototype, that they pointed to a common origin, and that all their differences must be accounted for either by Phonetic Corruption, or by Dialectic Growth. The comparative study of these languages became therefore genealogical, or, in grammatical phraseology, etymological.

Starting from a certain number of radical and formal elements (the latter being frequently radical elements of an earlier period), the principal object of the genealogical or etymological school has always been to discover the system according to which these elements were combined into words, and to determine the laws which regulate the phonetic changes of words, either in the same or in different languages. When these laws are sometimes treated as natural laws, this means in reality no more than that they admit of no exception, except such as can again be accounted for by new laws.
Analogical School.
The next school, the analogical, or as it might also E e % 420 LECTURE XVI.

be called, the dialectic, tries to discover what in the same or in different languages is not identical, but yet analogous. While the genealogical school looks upon all cognate languages as dialects developed from one ideal Koivrj, the dialectic school looks upon each language as the result of a previous independent growth, and is thus able to account for freedom and variety in single languages as well as in whole families of speech, as against the iron laws of phonetic change, established by the etymological school.

It would be impossible, for instance, or at allevents undesirable1, to treat say the Ionic dialect as a corruption of the Aeolic, or the Aeolic as a corruption of the Ionic. The same applies to High German and Low German, to Sanskrit and Prakrit, to Cymric and Goidhelic. These are all independent streams of language, which it is as hopeless to trace back to one common source, as it is to discover the one small source of the Nile, or even of the Thames. They spring indeed from the same geological stratum, and they follow parallel courses under similar conditions, but they are not yet one stream of water or of speech, kept in by the same shores and moving on in the same bed. Even after their confluence, the peculiar colours of what I call Dialectic Growth remain, and help us to account, whether by true or by false analogy, for that want of uniformity or regularity which the etymological school postulates with unyielding severity.

Thus dvau in Sanskrit, hvu in Greek, duo in Latin are phonetic varieties of one and the same type. They are identical in origin, and their differences can be 1 See Lectures on the Science of Language, vol. i. p. 55 seq.

MYTHOLOGY. 421 accounted for by phonetic laws. But Sk. dvitiya, the second, and Greek bevrepos are not identical in origin. They are dialectic forms, sprung from the same etymological stratum, not the products of one and the same creative act.

Nevertheless, it is in cognate languages only that we could account for such words as Sk. p rath a ma, the first, Greek irpwro?, Latin primus, and Gothic frvma. These are all analogous formations, only they must not be treated as varieties of one common prototype. Their differences are not due to the influence of phonetic modification, which can be reduced to a law, but to the freedom of dialectic growth, which must be accepted as a fact.

I go even further. We can hardly doubt, for instance, that the words for twenty' were formed by a composition of words meaning two and ten. In Chinese sM is ten, eul is two, therefore eul-sln, twenty. Our own twenty comes from Anglo-Saxon tuSn-tig, which corresponds to the Gothic tvai tigjus, and to the modern German zwanzig.

In Sanskrit we should expect a form like d vi-dasa, and in Latin duo-decem. But instead of this we find in Sanskrit vimsati, in Latin vigi nti, in Greek et/coo-i, and the older form Fzikciti. According to strict phonetic laws, these forms are all irregular. Dvi does never lose the initial d in Sanskrit, nor does dvi in Latin become vi, or in Greek el. In Sanskrit dvi ought to have remained ; in Greek dvi ought to have become 81, in Latin di or hi. Yet the fact remains that in one of the ancient Aryan dialects dvi was replaced by vi, for thus and thus only can we explain vi in vimsati, el in Greek in eiKoo-t, vi in Latin in 422 LECTURE XVI.
viginii. The stem for ten or decad, namely das a or dasat, was shortened to sat1, which is likewise without any phonetic excuse or analogy.

Here then we see what I call dialectic influence, as different from the independent working of phonetic laws. Vimsati is not a phonetic corruption of fet/cari of *Sf i + be Kan, but they are dialectic forms in which some old compound of twice-ten was fixed and retained, and was afterwards modified according to the peculiar phonetic instincts of Sanskrit, Greek, and Latin.

I cannot enter more fully into this subject at present, but I may remark that it is the disregard of this distinction between phonetic modification and dialectic growth which at the present moment seems to me to have led to a series of misunderstandings between the most prominent representatives of Comparative Philology2.
Psychological School.

The comparison of various languages, after it had led to the discovery of the great families of human speech and settled the principles according to which cognate languages should be analysed and explained, opened in the end a still wider prospect and disclosed before our eyes, not only what was common to Greek and Latin, to Hebrew and Arabic, to Finnish and Hungarian, but what was common and essential to all languages, what constitutes in fact the nature of language in general, and indirectly the nature of thought.
1 See Science of Language, i. p. 47.
3 G. Curtius, Zur Kritik der neuesten Sprachforschung, 18ST> ; Delbriick, Die Xeueste Sprachforschung. 1885; Brugmaim, Zum hmtigen Stand o'er Sprachivissenschaft, 1885.

MYTHOLOGY. 423 This kind of study, comparative in the widest sense, though it aimed at the discovery of the highest philosophical truth, does not depend for that discovery on abstract reasoning, but, differing thereby from all former attempts to construct a science of general grammar and of logic, it takes its materials entirely from the facts supplied by that infinite number of languages in which the power of language and thought has become realised. It matters little whether we call this branch of Comparative Philology psychological or ethno-psy etiological, as long as we see clearly that it aims at explaining that intellectual development which has its outward form in language, and that it derives its materials entirely from a careful study of the different types of human speech, so far as they are still accessible to the student of the present clay. To me, that branch of the Science of Language seems almost to transcend the powers of the present generation, and to belong to the future of our race. But I look to it as the final consummation of all that has ever claimed the name of philosophy, as the solution of all psychological, logical, and metaphysical problems, and in the end as the only true key to our knowledge of the Self.
Comparative Mythologfy.

What applies to Comparative Philology, applies mutatis mutandis to Comparative Mythology. I do not mean to say that the science of Comparative Mythology is as yet so firmly established as the Science of Comparative Philology. There is an honest difference of opinion with regard to many minor points, but the fundamental principles of Com- 424 LECTURE XVI.

parative Mythology, such as I tried to lay them down in my first essay on Comparative Mythology in the year 1856, are now generally admitted. I say generally, I do not say universally. There are still some philosophers who deny that the languages of the Greeks and Hindus, and the mythology of the Greeks and Hindus have anything in common ; but I do not know of any scholar of any authority who denies that the Greek Zeus finds its true explanation in the Vedic Dyaus, and that our first duty as students of Comparative Mythology must be to discover the etymology of as many mythological proper names as possible. To say that critics disagree among themselves, and that they need not be listened to till they agree, is one of those lazy commonplaces which no true scholar would dare to employ. I know full well that several mythological etymologies have been contested, and I have always been most grateful for any criticisms proceeding from scholars who really care for the progress of our science.
A. Barth on Comparative Mythology.

Among them few have a better right to be listened to than M. A. Barth. He has often criticised what Kuhn and others have written on the orioin of mythology, but after making all necessary reservation, he sums up as follows : —

' No one contests any longer that myths are from the first the natural and popular expression of very simple facts ; that particularly the most ancient have reference to the most common phenomena of nature ; that they depend very closely on language, being often no more than an antiquated form of it ; and MYTHOLOaY. 425 that what applies to words in general applies likewise to the immense variety of myths, namely that they can be reduced to a small number of elements, as words are to a small number of roots. In spite of the state of flux they are in, and their apparent confusion, they possess a certain cohesion and are held together by a kind of hidden logic. They do not migrate so easily nor so wildly as had been supposed, from one nation to another, from one race to another, but, like language, they are transmitted by inheritance only, and there are characteristic signs by which borrowed foreign myths can be discovered quite as well as borrowed foreign words. ... By applying these principles students of comparative mythology have established the fact that the common ancestors of the Celts, the Italians, the Hellenes, the Germans, the Slaves, the Iranians and the Hindus, at a time when they were still settled side by side in some for ever forgotten region of the old continent, adored the same deities ; and they have succeeded in restoring at least some persons of that prehistoric pantheon. Of these two series of results, which together constitute Aryan Mythology, the one, that which establishes the unity of beliefs, is certain, quite as certain as the corresponding result established by the Science of Language, namely the unity of an Indo-European mother tongue. The other series, however, the partial restoration of those beliefs, is far less certain.'

If it is considered that this judgment was delivered by a very independent judge so far back as 1880 in the Bulletin Critique de la Mythologie Aryenne in the Revue de VHistoire ties Rrtigions, it can hardly be called exaggeration when in 1886 I expressed my con- 426 LECTURE XV T.
viction that the fundamental principles of Comparative Mythology were now beyond the reach of cavil or criticism.

But in order to avoid misunderstandings and barren discussions, we ought to divide Comparative Mythology also into three branches, which may be defined as, (1) the Etymological or genealogical, (2) the Analogical, (3) the Psychological or ethno-psychological.
The Etymological School.

The Etymological branch of Comparative Mythology places the names and stories of certain gods and heroes side by side, and tries to prove that these names were derived from prototypes common to certain families of speech. As its object is not only to compare, but to identify these names, and the persons to whom they belong, it is clear that this branch of Comparative Mythology can deal with the traditions of such languages only as have been proved to be connected genealogically. It is natural, therefore, that this special domain of research should have been almost exclusively cultivated by critical scholars, and that the evidence to which they appeal should be entirely etymological, and under the sway of the strictest phonetic laws.
Analogical School.

The second branch, the Analogical, might claim for itself the principal right to the name of Comparative Mythology, for it is chiefly occupied with comparing myths and legends, without attempting to prove that they are actually identical in origin. Like the etymological school, it confines itself to the myths of cognate languages, but after having shown how many different MYTHOLOGY. 427 names and personifications may attach themselves to the principal objects of mythological thought, such as the sun. the moon, the sky, the earth, fire, and water, storms and lightning, and in how many different ways the same story may be told of these polynomous objects, it proceeds to a comparison of myths which, though not identical, must have sprung from the same common stratum, and thus takes possession of a far larger area of mythological thought as the common property of a race than could be claimed by purely etymological tests. This analogical process has its dangers, like all purely morphological comparisons, but it forms nevertheless an almost indispensable supplement in the genealogical treatment of mythology.
Psychological School.

While both the Genealogical and the Analogical schools confine themselves to a comparison of mythologies which are handed down to us in languages held together by the ties of a common origin, the Psychological or Ethno-psychological school soars higher, and comprehends the mythologies of all mankind. There is nothing in all the mythologies of the world that cannot be compared. What Heine said to an Ethno-psychological lover, Und mem Herz. was dir gefallt, Alles, Alles, darfst Du lieben' —
may be said to an Ethno-psychological Mythologist :
Und mein Freund, was dir gefallt. Alles, Alles, darfst vergleichen.'
It is a most fascinating, though, no doubt, at the same time, a somewhat dangerous study, unless it is carried on by men of scholarlike instinct and historical 428 LECTURE XVI.

tact. Its charm consists in the discovery of the most surprising coincidences in the mythologies, the customs, and traditions of distant races, distant in space as well as in time, unconnected by any relationship, whether genealogical or linguistic, civilised and uncivilised, ancient and modern. And it becomes still more attractive when it leads us on to the discovery of general motives which alone can account for such similarities. It becomes, in fact, an historical psychology of the human race (V other psychologie), and promises in time results of the highest value, not only to the historian, but to the philosopher also.
I. The Etymological School. The Names of Gods.

Comparative Mythology rests, as we saw, and can only rest, on Comparative Philology, and such has been the constant advance of that science, particularly with regard to the laws which regulate the interchange of consonants and vowels, that many etymological identifications which seemed quite legitimate fifty years ago, cannot be considered so any longer. My own conviction has always been that phonetic laws cannot be administered in too Draconian a spirit, and that there ought to be no difference made in applying them either to vowels or to consonants. It is far better to leave an etymology, however tempting, as not proven for a time, than to tamper with a single phonetic law.

But, with regard to mythological names, I confess that I myself have been guilty sometimes of pleading for circonstances attenuantes, and I must do so once more. I pointed out many years ago, first, that all MYTHOLOGY.

mythology was in its origin local or dialectic, and that therefore we must be prepared in mythological names for dialectic variations, which we should not tolerate in other nouns and verbs. In one of my latest papers (InternatioTiale Zeitschrift fur allgemeine Sprach urissenschaft, vol. i. p. 214), where I compare Zephyvos with the Vedic Gahusha, I had to remark : ' Scholars might differ as to Sanskrit <j being represented by Greek f ; but that on Greek soil y and C vary dialectically, can be seen from yevaavdai and (zvo-a(T0ai, einCapeio by the side of (3apvs, Sanskrit guru; TTcf)vCoT9 and TT<fiv yores,' etc.

I quite appreciate the motives which have led some scholars to dispute this principle and to object to the granting of any greater latitude in the comparison of mythological names than of ordinary words. But we cannot shut our eyes to facts. Now it is a fact that many of the Greek mythological names appear under dialectic forms which it would be difficult to reduce to general phonetic laws.
Dialectic varieties.

By the side of 'AvoMwv we find 'AttKovv (Thessalian), and in Etruscan Apia ; by the side of UoaeLb&v, Uoo-eCb-qs, Uortbas, II ore loclvv ; by the side of f,At8?/s, gen. f'Aibov, 'A 1677 j, gen. 'Aibao (also an old gen. vAt5os) ; by the side of Atj/uwjtijp, AS, A?]co. These are parallel or dialectic forms which cannot be derived one from the other. We cannot derive, for instance, Poseidon from Pot idas, nor Potidas from Poseidon. Supposing then that the form Potidas had not been preserved to us, but that Poseidon would yield an intelligible etymology if only we could trace it back to a form like Poteidon, we should have taken a phonetic liberty which might 430 LECTURE XVI.

be without any excuse in Greek, and which nevertheless might have yielded an explanation of the myth of Poseidon in accordance with facts. If we take even so well-known a name as Zets, I doubt whether its various forms, such as Zefc, gen. Al6s, Z&p, ZrjvSs, Ais and Aefa, could have been traced satisfactorily to a common base without the help of Sanskrit.
Obsolete Names.

Secondly, I pointed out, likewise many years ago, that it was almost an essential condition, before a name could assume a truly mythological character, that, by some accident or other, its etymological meaning should have been somewhat obscured. Words like Hemera, day, Nyx, night, Helios, sun, belene, moon, may send out a few mythological offshoots, but it is chiefly round dark and decaying names such as Kastor and Polydeukes, Apollon and Athene that the mythological ivy grows most luxuriantly 1.
The Dawn.

Let us now return for a moment to Eos. Her name in Sanskrit is Ushas, and we saw that it means the bright one, from a root VAS5 to shine. The Dawn. of course, might have been called by many names, and we shall soon see that she was called by many names and that some of them have survived though under strange disguises. But that Ushas should have remained her principal name in Sanskrit, and that the identical name, Eos, should appear in Greek, shows that she was known as a definite 'personality before the Aryan Separation.

1 See Benfey, Tritoma Athai '<-(, pp. s-y MYTHOLOGY. 431 A comparison of Eos, as we saw her in Greece, with what the Veda tells us of Us has, will show us that much in her character, which we are inclined to look upon as simply Greek poetry, is far more than that — is in fact the outcome of Aryan thought before it was divided into various national streams. Though I am rather anticipating, yet I think that a study of Us has, as she actually appears in the Veda, may prove useful as giving you a clear idea of what Comparative Mythology can really do for us.
We read, Rv. I. 48, 7: 'She has harnessed (her steeds) from a far distance, from the rising of the sun; on a hundred chariots Ushas spreads herself out, o-oinotowards men.'
Rv. I. 124, 5 : 'In the eastern half of the watery sky she has put forth her banner ; she spreads far and wide, and fills the two laps of her parents.'
You see how mythology is beginning. She has already chariot and horses, she carries a banner of light, and she has two parents, heaven and earth, whose lap she fills with her light.

In the same verse she is also called gavam r/anitri, the mother of the cows,' these cows being either the clouds which pour water on the fields, or the bright mornings which, like cows, are supposed to step out one by one from the stable of the night.

Thus we read again, Rv. III. 61, 4: 'Ushas, the mistress of the stable, comes, the wealthy, as if loosening her girdle.' Here she has clearly assumed the character of a woman, she has a girdle which she can put off like the clouds that surround her, and she is a rich woman, the mistress of her own stable, the stable constituting the chief wealth of the VedicAryas.

Thus she goes on growing before our very eyes. Sometimes she is still simply light, or like streams of light, or like rivers of clouds. Then it is said that she has no feet, and that she became the first of those who have feet (Rv. I. 152, 3), nay, that she is like a beautiful woman (Rv. I. 48, 5, yosha-iva sunari). Soon she is compared to a bride, adorned by her mother (1. 123 10. 11); as smiling on approaching her beloved (I, 123, 10 ; VII. 76, 3). Sometimes she is clothed in bright garments (I. 192, 1-4) ; at other times, proud of her faultless limbs, she reveals her beautiful body (I. 1 24 6; 1.123, 10; V. 80,5).

One feature peculiar to the Veda is the plurality of the dawn. Whether the many dawns were meant for the manifold rays of light playing across the sky in the morning, or for the dawns that come and go every day, certain it is that by the side of the one Dawn, poets constantly speak of the many Dawns also.
Religious Germs.

So far, however, all that is said of Ushas in the Veda might be called mere mythological poetry. But there are some expressions which contain religious germs. It is often said that the dawn is the sister of the dark night, that the two, day and night, succeed each other regularly, that the dawn is in fact always the same, always returning, the old, and yet the everyoung. Then follow such sayings as that she who makes every one else to grow old, remains young herself. Soon she is called the young, the never-aging, the never-dying — and at last, the immortal.

This will show you how language, by the mere formation of a certain class of words, leads us on to myth, MYTHOLOGY. 433 and from myth to religion. Ushas, the bright, devi, has now become Ushas, the immortal, and after that step has been taken, what is more natural than that she should become an attractive centre for other religious sentiments and thoughts ? Even with us a bright morning raises our spirits, and rouses a sense of happiness and gratitude in our heart, though the object of our gratitude may remain nameless. Think what it must have been in early times, when life and everything was felt to depend on the kindly light of the morning ! A bright sunrise was a new life, a sunless, cold, stormy morning meant suffering, often starvation or even death. Need we wonder then that some words should have been stammered forth at the rise of a bright dawn, words of joy and gratitude, addressed not to a nameless being, but to the kind and brilliant Ushas, or Eos, or Aurora 1 Moral Germs.

Moral ideas also would soon cluster round such names as the Dawn. If a crime, a dark crime, as we call it, had been committed during the night, who was to discover or to revenge it ? Again the Dawn, under one of her many names, the Sanskrit 8ara//yu, the Greek Erinys.

You may say, But why did not people ask who that Eos really was, before they praised her and thanked her ? This is a difficult question to answer. But do we always ask what a name really means, what is behind a name, what is the true substance of a name ? We speak of Angels, without asking what they are made of. The ancients did the same, and when they had called Ushas, the daughter of Dyaus or Zeus, their F f 434 lecture xvr.

mind seemed satisfied, at least for a time. Names have a wonderfully satisfying power, and few only venture to lift the veil which language has thrown over nature. And when they do, what do they find 1 They find the infinite hidden under a name, and they find that all they can know of the infinite is what is signified by these names. Ushas, the morning light, is as good a name for the Infinite as Dyaus, the sky, who became Zeus and Jupiter, only that its history took a different direction. And remember that we ourselves also, though we may no longer use the name of Morninglight for the Infinite, the Beyond, the Divine, still find no better expression than Light, when we speak of the manifestations of God whether in nature or in our mind.
Ahana, Athene.
So far the way of Comparative Mythology is smooth and easy. But etymology, if only kept under proper control, can lead us over more rugged roads, and give us light in darker passages.

I said before that though Ushas was the oldest name of the Dawn, having been fixed before the Aryan Separation, there were many other names given to the same phenomenon, as looked upon from different points of view. Some of these names might be used by one poet only, others might become traditional in one family or clan and these dialectic names would lend themselves most easily to mythological phraseology, on account of the very uncertainty of their original meaning. The dawn as Ushas has become mythological, but, as we saw in the Veda as well as in Homer, its natural character was never quite forgotten.

Now there is in the Veda another name for the MYTHOLOGY. 435 Dawn, which is Ah ana. It occurs but once, in a hymn addressed to Ushas, and there can be no doubt that it is one of the many epithets of the dawn.

I. 123. 4. GWham-gn'ham ahana yati dkkha. Dive-dive adhi nama dadhiina, Sisasanti dyotana sasvat a agat Agram-agram it bhagate vasunam. ' Ahana comes towards every house, Giving a name to every day ; Dyotana returns always eager for gain. She obtains the best of all treasures.'

When we ask why Ahana should mean the dawn, the answer is easy. A h a n and a h a r mean the day, and ahani in the dual means day and night. In Sanskrit mythology this name of Ahana has remained sterile, but in Greek, as we shall see, it has become the germ of a magnificent growth. When we ask under what form Ahana could appear in Greek we should say at first-'Axa^a or 'Ax^a. Neither of these forms exists. But we must remember that Sanskrit h represents three original sounds, namely gh, dh, and bh. It represents gh, for instance, in dah,to burn, by the side of which we have Sanskrit ni-dagha, heat. It represents d h, as, for instance, in N AH. to bind together, nectere, by the side of which we have *NADH, in the present naddha. It represents bh, as in the same root NAH, by the side of which we have NABH in nabhi, in GRAH and GRABH, both meaning to take, to grab l.
In Greek itself we find the aspirates changing dialectically. We have not only dpi'ts, opm6o?< but also opvis, dpvixos. Wre have Wfxa and Ixfia, and similar forms.
1 Cf. Sk. dahra = dab bra. F f % 436 LECTURE XVI.

We have therefore a perfect right to expect Athand or Aphand instead of Achand1. Now 'kd&va exists in Greek as an old name of Athene. We have also ' Adavai, and 'AOdraCa. In Athene we have the same suffix as in Selene, and the change between the two suffixes ana and ana has been shown to be very common 2. Phonetically therefore the identification of Ah an a in the Veda and Athene in Greek is beyond the reach of criticism and cavil. If after that we identify Ah ana with Athene mythologically also, we must see clearly what we mean. First of all, we cannot mean that there ever was a real being, a woman or a goddess, who was known in India and in Greece and had received there the same name, Ah ana and Athand.
Secondly, we cannot mean that whatever was told of Athene in Greek was told of Ahana also in Sanskrit.
Thirdly, and least of all, can we mean that the worship of Ahana was carried from India to Greece, or the worship of Athene from Greece to India.

All we can mean is that Ahana, as a name of the dawn, was known before Greek and Sanskrit separated, and that while in India this mythological germ withered away, it developed into a splendid growth in Greece.

We see the same with common words. Bha(/, for instance, in Sanskrit, means to divide, and one of the Vedic gods, Bhaga, meant originally the divider and benefactor. In Zend also Baga appears in the same character, and in the Slavonic languages the Old Slav, bogii has become the general name for god. It is often urged by Greek scholars that the Greeks themselves had no idea that Athene meant originally the dawn, or the verb Qaydv, to divide. That, no doubt, is true, and it is quite as true that few only of the Greeks knew that Zeus meant originally the sky, and Zephyros the wind blowing from the setting of the sun, or Boreas the wind blowing from the northern mountains. We do not know that Lord meant originally bread-giver, or Duke a man of leading and light ; but it is only after knowing it that we can understand the historical growth of the later meanings of Lord and Duke.

Nor is it impossible to discover certain traces in the mythological stories told of Athene which point to her original character as dawn-goddess. Her birth from the head of Zeus is like the rising of the dawn in the Veda from the head of Dyaus (murdha DivaA) ; and it may be in the same sense that she was called Koryphasia, as coming k Kopvcf)^1, and that her counterpart in Italy was called Cap(i)ta. But what is most interesting to the student of mythology, the germinal idea of the goddess, can be found nowhere else but in the name of Ah an a, which would have been forgotten in India also, if it had not been for the single verse of the Rig-veda which I quoted to you.

Whether the two roots, AH, from which ah an, day, and DAH, from which Goth, dags1, day, are parallel roots, is a question that can only be decided by a full discussion of general principles. 神話學。417 神話的普遍性。

長期以來，人們認為我們所謂的神話學大多源於希臘人獨特的詩歌天才。我們對神話學的初次認識來自希臘，從學生時代起就習慣於將希臘人視為一個天賦異稟的民族，因此我們認為可以放心地將他們神話中所有的美麗和智慧的發明歸功於他們。那幅光明圖景中確實存在著黑暗面，這一點無法否認；但那些不諳其他民族神話學的古典學者們認為，古典神話中所有令人厭惡和愚蠢之處，都可以解釋為蠻荒時代的遺存，當時希臘人和羅馬人的蠻荒祖先並不避諱犯下那些他們後來慈愛地歸咎於諸神的罪行和愚舉。
正是在這裡，比較神話學發揮了作用，幫助我們解決了許多無法透過任何其他理論來消除的困難。

比較神話學（Comparative Mythology）是宗教學（the science of religion）的一個分支，它與比較神學（comparative theology）並列，共同構成這門新興學科的兩大支柱。正如語言科學（the science of language）透過比較不同語言的語法與詞根，揭示了人類語言能力的統一性與發展規律，比較神話學也試圖透過比較不同民族的宗教觀念與神話敘事，追溯人類宗教意識的源頭與演變。 穆勒（Müller）指出，神話並非純粹的虛構或欺騙，而是人類心智在遠古時期對自然現象的一種必然反應。當原始人類面對無限者（the Infinite）——如天空、太陽、雷電、風雨——時，他們無法以抽象概念來表達這些力量，於是便藉助具體的感官詞彙來描述。然而，隨著語言的演變，這些原本具有明確意義的詞彙逐漸失去了原意，後代的人們便將其誤解為獨立的神祇與故事。這就是穆勒著名的「語言的疾病」（a disease of language）理論。 在雅利安（Aryan）民族的早期文獻中，我們可以看到這種現象的痕跡。例如，在吠陀（Veda）的《梨俱吠陀》（Rig-Veda）中，天空之神特尤斯（Dyaus）與其他提婆（Deva）原本只是自然現象的擬人化稱呼。透過比較神話學的方法，我們可以追溯這些神祇名稱在不同語言中的對應關係，從而重建原始雅利安人的宗教觀念。這種比較研究也揭示了從多神教（polytheism）到單一神教（henotheism）或輪換主神教（kathenotheism），再到一神教（monotheism）的發展脈絡。 此外，比較神話學不僅限於雅利安語系，也涵蓋閃語族（Semitic）與圖蘭語族（Turanian）的傳統。透過對《奧義書》（Upanishad）等經典的分析，我們得以理解不同文化如何以神話形式表達對無限者的追尋。
比較神話學及其三分法
比較一詞乃是近來幾乎被所有歷史科學與自然科學所採用的名稱，然而，一旦我們真正理解任何單一科學的真正方法與目的，以此謂詞來限定它似乎幾乎是多餘的。天下並無單一之物的科學，人類知識的一切進步皆係通過比較而成就，比較引導我們發現不同物件所共有者，直至我們達到最廣泛的普遍化為止。
以及人類知識範圍內所能達及的最高理念。

比較語言學（Comparative Philology）這門科學，在過去五十年間所取得的輝煌成就，為我們提供了研究宗教學（the science of religion）不可或缺的基礎。正如語言科學（the science of language）使我們得以追溯雅利安（Aryan）、閃語族（Semitic）與圖蘭語族（Turanian）諸語言之血脈，直至其最古老的源頭，宗教學亦將引領我們沿著相似的路徑，去探索宗教觀唸的起源與發展。當我們比較吠陀（Veda）中對特尤斯（Dyaus）的呼喚、希臘人對宙斯（Ζεύς）的祈禱，以及羅馬人對朱庇特（Jupiter）的禮讚時，我們便會發現，這些看似迥異的神祇，實則源於同一祖先的模糊記憶。這種比較方法，不僅揭示了多神教（polytheism）的起源，更讓我們得以窺見，在那些被稱為「單一神教（henotheism）」或「輪換主神教（kathenotheism）」的古老崇拜形式中，一神教（monotheism）的種子是如何悄然萌發的。
因此，就語言而言，我們對於詞彙知識的最初步驟乃是通過比較而成。語法又何嘗不是由許多詞彙組成的一個集合體——雖然這些詞彙彼此不同，卻共享某些形式上的共同要素？這些形式上的要素被稱為語法要素，或是字尾、詞綴、字首等等，而當我們學會了在什麼條件下不同的詞彙會經歷相同的形式變化時，我們就被認為懂得了一種語言的語法。
因此，比較在首先導向對單一語言的文法認識。
然而，當我們從研究單一語言進而研究眾多語言之時，一個嶄新的比較過程隨之開展。我們觀察到不同語言中的詞彙經歷相同或幾乎相同的變化，並且通過將它們的格變和動詞變位範例並列放置，我們努力找出它們在哪些要點上相符，在哪些要點上有異，並期望藉此最終發現它們之所以在某些要點上相符，以及它們之所以在其他要點上有異的理由。
比較語文學一方面涉及事實——即可在語言的物質與形式要素中觀察到的差異與巧合；另一方面涉及規律——此處使用「規律」一詞係採取謙遜的意義，即「對許多事物都成立的某種事物」，而非如某些學者所想像的那種意義。這些規律旨在說明賦予各語言其獨特性格的種種特殊性。
然而，這門比較語言學很快就呈現出三種不同的面貌，並在三個流派中得到開展，這些流派可稱為：（1）詞源學派或譜系學派，（2）類比學派，及（3）心理學派。
語源學派
在比較拉丁文、希臘文和梵文等語言時，人們很快就發現它們實際上只是同一個歷史原型的各種變體，它們指向一個共同的起源，而它們的所有差異必須歸因於**語音訛變**或**方言演變**。因此，這些語言的比較研究便成為系譜學的，或用文法術語來說，是**詞源學的**研究。
從一定數量的根本要素和形式要素出發（後者往往是較早時期的根本要素），系譜學派或詞源學派的主要目標始終是發現這些要素如何結合成詞語的系統，並釐定規範詞語音韻變化的法則，無論是在同一語言或不同語言中皆然。當這些法則有時被視為自然法則時，實際上不過是說它們不容例外，除非這些例外又可以用新的法則來解釋。
比喻學派
下一個學派是類比派（analogical），或者也可以稱為
被稱為辯證法（dialectic）的學派，則試圖發現在相同或不同語言中那些非完全相同、卻仍具類比性的特徵。當系譜學派將所有同源語言視為由某一理想的共同語（Koivrj）演化而來的方言時，辯證法學派則將每一種語言視為先前獨立發展的產物，因此能夠解釋單一語言與整個語言族系中所存在的自由性與多樣性——相對於詞源學派所建立的語音變化的鐵律而言。

舉例來說，把愛奧尼亞方言視為埃奧利亞方言的變異，或反過來把埃奧利亞方言視為愛奧尼亞方言的變異，這樣做要麼根本不可能，要麼至少是不可取的¹。同樣的道理也適用於高地德語和低地德語、梵語和俗語、威爾斯語和蓋爾語。這些都是語言的獨立支流，要將其追溯至一個共同源頭，就如同要發現尼羅河甚至泰晤士河的唯一源頭一樣，同樣是徒勞無功的。它們確實源於同一個地質層，並在相似的條件下循著平行的路線流動，但它們還不是同一股水流或同一股語言，受同一邊界所束縛，在同一河床中運行。即使在它們匯合之後，我所謂的「方言成長」(Dialectic Growth) 的獨特特徵仍然保留下來，並幫助我們透過真正的或虛假的類比，來解釋語言學派所執著堅持的那種不規則性或不一致性的現象。
因此，梵文的 dvau、希臘文的 hvu、拉丁文的 duo 都是同一類型的語音變體。它們在起源上是同一的，其差異可以 ¹ 參見《語言科學講座》第一卷，第 55 頁及以下。
神話學。421 這可由音韻規律來說明。但梵文 dvitiya（第二）與希臘文 δεύτερος 並非源出同一。它們是方言形式，源自同一語源地層，而非源自同一創造行為的產物。
儘管如此，只有在同族語言中，我們才能說明如梵文 prath a ma（第一）、希臘文 πρῶτος、拉丁文 primus 和哥德文 frvma 這樣的詞彙。這些都是類似的構詞，但它們不應被視為某一共同原型的變體。它們的差異並非源於可歸納為規律的音韻變化的影響，而是源於方言發展的自由性，這必須被接受為一個事實。
我甚至更進一步。例如，我們幾乎不能懷疑「二十」這個詞是由表示「二」和「十」的詞語複合而成的。在中文中，十是「十」，二是「二」，因此「二十」就是二十。我們自己的 twenty 來自盎格魯‧撒克遜語的 twain-tig，這與哥德語的 tvai tigjus 相應，並與現代德語的 zwanzig 相同。

在梵文中，我們應當預期像 d vi-dasa 這樣的形式，在拉丁文中則應是 duo-decem。但實際上，在梵文中我們發現的是 vimsati，在拉丁文中是 viginti，在希臘文中是 εἴκοσι，以及更古老的形式 Fzικοσι。根據嚴格的音韻法則，這些形式全都是不規則的。梵文中的 dvi 從不失去首字母 d，拉丁文中的 dvi 也不會變成 vi，希臘文中的 dvi 亦不會變成 εἴ。在梵文中 dvi 本應保留下來；在希臘文中 dvi 本應變成 δί；在拉丁文中應是 di 或 hi。然而事實仍然存在，即在古代雅利安某一方言中，dvi 被 vi 所取代，因為唯有如此，我們才能解釋梵文 vimsati 中的 vi、希臘文 εἴκοσι 中的 εἴ，以及拉丁文中的 vi。
viginii. 十或十進制的詞根，即 das a 或 dasat，被縮短為 sat，這同樣缺乏任何音韻學的藉口或類比根據。
於此我們可以看到我所謂的方言影響，這與音韻律則的獨立作用截然不同。Vimsati 並非 fet/cari 或 *Sf i + be Kan 的音韻訛變，而是若干表達「二十」的古老複合詞所定著與保留的方言形式，其後依照梵文、希臘文和拉丁文各自特有的音韻傾向而有所更動。
我目前無法更深入地討論這個課題，但我必須指出，目前在最傑出的比較語言學代表人物之間似乎正是由於忽視了音位變化（phonetic modification）與方言發展（dialectic growth）之間的區別，才導致了一系列的誤解。
心理學學派
各種語言的比較，在發現了人類語言的各大族系並確立了同源語言應如何被分析和解釋的原則之後，最終打開了更加寬闊的視野，向我們展現了不僅存在於希臘語與拉丁語、希伯來語與阿拉伯語、芬蘭語與匈牙利語之間的共通之處，而且揭示了對所有語言都共有且本質的東西——實際上構成了語言本性的東西，並間接構成了思想本性的東西。
見《語言科學》第一冊第47頁。
3 G. Curtius,《最近語言研究之批評》，1851；Delbrück,《最近語言研究》，1885；Brugmann,《語言科學之當前狀況》，1885。

這種研究，在最廣泛的意義上是比較性的，雖然它旨在發現最高的哲學真理，但其發現並不依賴於抽象推理，從而有別於所有先前構建一般語法科學和邏輯學的嘗試。它完全從那無限數量的語言所提供的事實中汲取材料，這些語言是人類語言與思維的力量所實現的形式。我們是否稱比較語文學的這一分支為心理學的或民族心理學的（ethno-psychological）意義不大，只要我們清楚地看到它旨在解釋那種具有語言作為其外部形式的智識發展，並且它完全從對人類言語的不同類型的仔細研究中汲取材料，只要這些類型對於當今時代的研究者仍然可以接近。在我看來，語言科學的這一分支似乎幾乎超越了當代人類的力量，而屬於我們人類的未來。但我將其視為曾經自稱為哲學的一切的最終成就，視為所有心理學、邏輯學和形而上學問題的解答，最終視為我們認識自我的唯一真正鑰匙。

我們現在必須轉向比較神話學，這門學科與比較神學的關係，正如語言科學與比較語法學的關係一般。然而，在我開始闡述比較神話學的基本原則之前，我必須先處理一個反對意見，這個反對意見或許已經在許多讀者心中浮現。有人可能會說：「神話學並非宗教；神話學不過是宗教的陰影，或者更糟，是宗教的疾病。」確實，神話學中有許多內容是我們無法認同的；但我們必須記住，神話學並非我們所創造的，而是人類心靈在特定發展階段中必然的產物。正如語言科學教導我們，語言中的所謂「語言的疾病」——即那些看似不規則、無意義的詞形變化——實際上揭示了語言成長的有機法則，同樣地，比較神話學也向我們展示，那些看似荒誕不經、褻瀆神明的神話，其實是早期人類試圖理解無限者時，其心智活動的必然結果。
比較語言學所適用的原則，經過適當的調整，也同樣適用於比較神話學。我並非要說比較神話學在目前已經像比較語言學那樣確立為一門堅實的科學。關於許多細節問題，確實存在著誠實的意見分歧，但比較神話學的基本原則——

比較神話學的原則，如同我在一八五六年第一篇關於比較神話學的論文中所試圖闡述的，現在已普遍為人接受。我說「普遍」，並非說「絕對普遍」。仍有少數哲學家否認希臘人與印度人的語言、希臘人與印度人的神話之間有任何共通之處；但我所知的任何有權威的學者都不會否認希臘的宙斯（Zeus）在吠陀的特尤斯（Dyaus）中找到其真正的解釋，以及我們作為比較神話學研究者的首要職責必定是發現盡可能多的神話專有名詞的詞源。說評論家彼此意見不合，因此無須聽取他們的意見直到他們達成共識為止，這種說法乃是那種真正的學者絕不敢採用的懶惰老調。我深知許多神話詞源學說曾遭質疑，我對於來自那些真正關心我們宗教學進展的學者所提出的任何批評，向來都心懷感激。
**A‧巴爾特論比較神話學**
在這些人中，很少有人比巴爾特先生（M. A. Barth）更有資格被傾聽。他經常批評庫恩及其他人對神話起源所寫的內容，但在作出所有必要的保留之後，他總結如下：——

沒有人再否認，神話自始即為極為簡單事實的自然而通俗的表達；特別是最古老的神話係涉及自然界最常見的現象；它們與語言有密切的關聯，往往不過是語言的古老形式；凡是適用於詞語一般者亦適用於眾多繁雜的神話，亦即它們可被化約為少數的若干要素，如同詞語可化約為少數的若干根基。儘管神話處於流變的狀態，表面上看似混亂，但它們具有某種凝聚力，由一種隱祕的邏輯所貫繫。它們不像人們所想的那樣容易、那樣無序地從一個民族傳往另一民族，從一個種族傳往另一種族，而是如同語言一樣，僅通過世代相傳而流布，並且存在著某些特徵標記，藉此可以識別出外來的借用神話，就如識別外來借用詞語一樣清楚。……藉由運用這些原則，比較神話學的研究者已經確立了這樣的事實：凱爾特人、義大利人、赫臘人、日耳曼人、斯拉夫人、伊朗人和印度人的共同祖先，在他們仍然相鄰而居於上古大陸某個永已遺忘之地區的時代，曾崇敬同樣的諸神；他們亦已成功地至少復原了那個史前萬神殿的某些神祇。這兩個結果序列合而成為雅利安神話學，其中之一即確立信仰一致性的結果，其確定性毫無疑問，如同語言科學所確立的相應結果一樣確定，即印歐母語的統一性。然而另一個結果序列，亦即那些信仰的部分復原，則遠非如此確定。
若考慮到這個判斷是由一位非常獨立的評判者早在1880年在《雅利安神話學批評公報》（Bulletin Critique de la Mythologie Aryenne）及《宗教歷史評論》（Revue de l'Histoire des Religions）上所發表的，那麼當我在1886年表達同樣看法時，實難謂之為誇大其詞。
比較神話學的基本原則已經超越了非議與批評的範圍，這一信念已然確立。
但為了避免誤解與徒勞無功的討論，我們應當將比較神話學也分為三個分支，其定義如下：（1）語源學的或譜系的、（2）類比的、（3）心理學的或民族心理學的。
語源學派
比較神話學的語源學分支，將某些神祇與英雄的名字和故事並列比較，並試圖證明這些名字源自於某些語族共有的原型。由於其目的不僅在於比較，更在於識別這些名字及其所代表的人物，因此很明顯地，比較神話學的這一分支只能處理那些已被證明在譜系上相互關聯的語言傳統。因此，這一特殊的研究領域自然地主要由批判性的學者所開墾，而他們所援引的證據應當完全是語源學性質的，並受到最嚴格的音韻法則的制約。
類比學派

第二個分支——類比法（Analogical）——可以據理主張它最有資格享有比較神話學（Comparative Mythology）這個名稱，因為它主要從事於神話與傳說的比較工作，而無須試圖證明它們實際上源於同一起源。如同語源學派一樣，它也將自身侷限於同源語言的神話之中；但在展示了多少不同的名稱與擬人化形象可以附著在神話思想的主要對象上——諸如太陽、月亮、天空、大地、火、水、風暴與閃電——以及同一故事可以以多少不同的方式講述這些多名對象之後，它進而比較那些雖非完全相同、卻必定源自同一共同基層的神話。因此它所掌握的神話思想範圍，作為一個種族的共同財產，遠較純粹語源學測試所能主張者為大。這種類比過程如同所有純粹形態學的比較一樣，具有其危險性；然而在神話的譜系學處理中，它卻構成了幾乎不可或缺的補充。
心理學派
而系譜學派與類比學派均局限於比較那些以共同起源的語言紐帶相互連結的神話學，心理學派或民族心理學派則飛得更高，涵攝全人類的神話學。世界上所有神話學中沒有任何東西不能被比較。海涅對一位民族心理學家的愛人所說的話——「凡是我的心所喜歡的，一切、一切，妳都可以去愛」——
可以對一位民族心理神話學家這樣說：
• 「我的朋友啊，凡合你心意之事，一切、一切，你都可以比較。」
這無疑是一項最具吸引力的研究，儘管同時也存在著某種危險性，除非從事此研究的人具有學者的直覺與歷史感 428 第十六講。
洞察力。它的魅力在於發現遠距離種族的神話、風俗與傳統中最令人驚奇的巧合，這些種族無論在空間上或時間上都相距遙遠，彼此毫無關連，既無系譜關係亦無語言關係，有文明的也有未開化的，古代的也有現代的。當它進而引領我們發現能夠解釋此等相似性的普遍動機時，它變得更加引人入勝。實際上，它成為了人類的歷史心理學（*andere psychologie*），並且承諾將在時日中為歷史學家和哲學家帶來最為重要的成果。
I. 語源學派。神的名字
比較神話學如我們所見，並且只能以比較語言學為基礎，而該科學的進展乃至其中特別關乎輔音與母音互換法則的知識進展，已達到如此地步，以至於五十年前看來完全合理的許多詞源學認定，如今已不復能視為有效。我個人向來的信念是，語音法則的應用不可過於寬鬆，在將之應用於母音或輔音時，應當不分彼此。寧可暫時將一個詞源學說認定為未經證實，儘管它引人入勝，也遠勝於動搖單一的語音法則。
但就神話名稱而言，我必須坦白，我自己有時曾為情狀輕微辯解，我必須再做一次。我許多年前曾指出，首先，所有神話。

神話在其起源上是地方性或方言性的，因此我們必須為神話名稱中的方言變異做好準備，而這種變異在其他名詞和動詞中是不應該容忍的。在我最近的一篇論文中（*Internationale Zeitschrift für allgemeine Sprachwissenschaft*, vol. i. p. 214），我將 Zephyros 與吠陀文的 Gahusha 進行比較時，曾經指出：「學者們在梵文 j 是否由希臘文 φ 來代表這一點上可能有分歧；但是在希臘土地上 γ 和 ζ 在方言上是變異的，這可以從 γεναάδαι 和 ζυo-ἀσται 看出來，埃卡佩里翁（einCapeia）與 βαρύς（梵文 guru）並列；πέφνζοτες 和 πέφυ yores」等等。
我完全理解促使某些學者質疑這一原則的動機，以及他們反對在神話名字的比較中給予比普通詞彙更大緯度的理由。然而，我們不能對事實視而不見。事實上，許多希臘神話名字呈現出多種方言形式，這些形式很難化約為普遍的音韻法則。
辯證的多樣性。

在 'AvoMwv 一側，我們發現 'AttKovv（色薩利方言），以及伊特魯里亞語中的 Apia；在 UoaeLb&v 一側，有 Uoo-eCb-qs、Uortbas、II ore loclvv；在 f,At8?/s（屬格 f'Aibov、'A 1677）一側，有 'A 1677j（屬格 'Aibao，亦為古屬格 vAt5os）；在 Atj/uwjtijp、AS、A?]co 一側。這些是平行的或方言形式，無法從彼此推導而出。例如，我們無法從 Pot idas 推導出 Poseidon，也無法從 Poseidon 推導出 Potidas。假若 Potidas 的形式未被保留給我們，但若 Poseidon 能在我們回溯到如 Poteidon 之類的形式時產生可理解的詞源，我們將採取一種語音上的自由做法，這可能……
在希臘語中完全無法辯解，儘管如此它本可以根據事實為波塞冬的神話提供解釋。即便以宙斯這樣一個眾所周知的名字為例，我也懷疑其各種形式，如 Zefc、Zefs（屬格）、Zήν、Zήνδς、Ais 和 Aefa，若無梵文的幫助，是否能令人滿意地溯源至共同的詞根。

當我們追溯各民族的宗教史時，我們發現許多神靈的名字已經完全消失，或者至少已經喪失了其原始的意義和力量。這些我稱之為「廢棄的神名」(obsolete names)，它們向我們提供了一個最清晰的視角，使我們得以洞察語言在宗教發展中所扮演的角色。 一個神名之所以會過時，往往是因為人們對其詞源的記憶已經淡忘。當最初賦予某位神祇的那個鮮活的描述性名稱變得陌生、晦澀或無法理解時，該神祇就開始衰退。這正是「語言的疾病」(a disease of language)在宗教領域中最顯著的表現。 例如，在雅利安民族的信仰中，許多古老的神名原本具有明確的自然象徵意義，但隨著時間推移，當信徒不再理解這些名字的原義時，這些神祇就逐漸淡出了人們的記憶。它們的祭祀被遺忘，它們的故事被遺棄，它們的名字變成了空洞的音聲。 這種現象對於比較神話學和比較神學的研究具有重大意義。廢棄的神名為我們保存了宗教演變過程中珍貴的痕跡，它們像化石一樣，向我們揭示了宗教發展的層次和人類精神信仰的流變。

其次，我早年亦曾指出，一個名稱欲能具有真正的神話學特徵，幾乎是一項根本條件，即其詞源學意義應當因某種偶然原因而有所晦暗。諸如赫美拉（Hemera，日）、尼克斯（Nyx，夜）、赫利俄斯（Helios，日）、貝琳涅（belene，月）之類的詞彙，或許可能衍生出些許神話的分枝；然而神話學的常春藤卻是繞著那些名義已然晦暗與衰落的詞彙，如卡斯托爾與波呂杜刻斯（Kastor and Polydeukes）、阿波羅與雅典娜（Apollon and Athene）而最為蓊鬱茂盛地蔓生1。
晨曦
讓我們現在暫時回到厄俄斯。她在梵文中的名字是烏莎斯（Ushas），我們看到它的意思是光明者，源自根詞 VAS「閃耀」。當然，曙光可能被賦予許多名字，我們很快就會看到她確實被稱呼過許多名字，其中有些在奇異的偽裝下倖存了下來。但烏莎斯在梵文中保持了她主要的名字，以及同一個名字厄俄斯（Eos）出現在希臘文中，這表明在雅利安民族分化之前，她就已被認識為一個明確的「個性」。

1 見 Benfey, Tritoma Athai '<-(, pp. s-y MYTHOLOGY. 431 對希臘神話中的厄俄斯（Eos）與吠陀（Veda）中所述的烏莎斯（Us has）進行比較，將向我們表明，許多我們傾向於將其視為純粹的希臘詩歌的特質，實際上遠不止於此——事實上是雅利安思想在分裂成各個民族支流之前的產物。雖然我略嫌超越主題，但我認為對烏莎斯在吠陀中的實際面貌進行研究，可能會對你有所幫助，使你能清楚地認識到比較神話學究竟能真正為我們做什麼。
我們在《梨俱吠陀》第一卷第四十八讚歌第七節讀到：「她自遠方駕馭（她的馬匹），自太陽升起之處；烏沙絲乘坐百輛戰車展開自己，o-oino-（朝向）眾人。」
《梨俱吠陀》第一卷 124 首 5 頌：「在水性蒼穹的東半部，她豎起了自己的旌旗；她廣為散布，充滿了雙親的兩懷。」
你看，神話學正在開始成形。她已經擁有戰車和馬匹，她揮舞著光明的旗幟，她有兩位雙親——天與地——她用她的光芒充滿了他們的懷抱。
在同一詩節中，她也被稱為「牲畜之母」（gavam r/anitri），這些牲畜要麼是那些在田地上傾瀉雨水的雲靄，要麼是那些如牲畜般據信一隻接一隻地從夜晚的牛棚踏出的光亮晨曦。
因此我們再次讀到《梨俱吠陀》第三卷第六一讚第四頌：「烏莎絲（Ushas），馬廄的女主人，富裕的她來臨，彷彿鬆開她的腰帶。」在此，她明確地呈現了女性的性格，她有一條腰帶可以脫去，就如同環繞著她的雲彩一樣，而且她是一位富有的女人，自己馬廄的女主人，馬廄構成了吠陀時代雅利安人的主要財富。

這樣她在我們眼前不斷成長。有時她仍然只是光，或如光的溪流，或如雲的河川。據說她沒有腳，而後來成為那些有腳者中的第一個（《梨俱吠陀》I. 152, 3），甚至說她如同一位美麗的女性（《梨俱吠陀》I. 48, 5, yosha-iva sunari）。不久她被比作新娘，為其母親所裝飾（I. 123, 10. 11）；如微笑著走向心愛的人（I. 123, 10；VII. 76, 3）。有時她穿著明亮的衣裳（I. 192, 1-4）；在其他時候，為自己完美無缺的肢體而自豪，她展露出自己美麗的身軀（I. 124, 6；I. 123, 10；V. 80, 5）。
吠陀的一個獨特特徵是曙光的複數性。無論眾多的曙光是指晨間光線在天空中綻放的繁多光芒，還是指每日往來的曙光，確定的是，在單一的曙光（Dawn）之外，詩人們經常也談及眾多的曙光（Dawns）。

宗教的萌芽 在我們的宗教學研究中，最為重要的是要認識到，所有的宗教都有其最簡單、最原始的形態。我們無法理解任何宗教的發展，除非我們首先回溯到其最初的根源，並觀察那些構成其基礎的基本原理。這些基本原理我稱之為「宗教的萌芽」（Religious Germs）。 每一種宗教的萌芽都源自於人類心靈對無限者（the Infinite）的一種最初的、最天然的認知。這種認知並非來自於理性的反思，而是源自於人在面對自然界的偉大現象時所產生的一種直覺的、本能的感受。當人類觀看星空、聆聽雷鳴、凝視深淵時，心中油然而生一種敬畏與神聖感；正是在這一刻，宗教的萌芽便在人的心靈中萌發了。 理解宗教的萌芽，對於從事比較神學（comparative theology）與比較神話學（comparative mythology）的學者而言，至關重要。唯有通過識別各民族宗教中的共同萌芽，我們才能夠辨別出宗教發展過程中哪些是普遍的、必然的演變，哪些又是特殊的、偶然的變異。
然而，迄今為止，吠陀中關於烏莎絲（Ushas）所言的一切都可以稱為純粹的神話詩歌。但其中有些表述蘊含著宗教的萌芽。人們常說晨曦是黑暗夜晚的姐妹，晝夜兩者有規律地相繼出現，晨曦實際上始終如一，不斷返回，既是古老的，卻又永遠年輕。隨後出現的說法是：她使他人衰老，自身卻保持年輕。不久她被稱為年輕者、永不衰老者、永不死亡者——最終，她成了永生者。

這將向你展示語言如何透過特定詞彙類別的單純構成，引導我們進入神話、**神話學** 433 ，以及從神話進入宗教。烏莎絲（Ushas），明亮者、提婆女神，如今已成為烏莎絲，不朽者；一旦這個步驟完成，還有什麼比她更自然地成為其他宗教情感與思想的吸引中心呢？即使在我們當中，明亮的晨曦也會振奮我們的心靈，喚起感謝與幸福的感受，雖然我們感謝的對象可能仍是無名的。想想在早期時代這必然是什麼樣的光景，當時生命及一切都被感受為依賴晨光的仁慈照拂！明亮的日出代表新的生命，無日光、寒冷、風暴肆虐的早晨意味著苦難，常常帶來飢荒甚至死亡。我們還需要驚異，為何在明亮晨曦初現時會有某些詞彙被喃喃說出——這些歡樂與感謝之詞，不是獻給無名之存有，而是獻給仁慈而燦爛的烏莎絲或厄俄斯（Eos）或奧羅拉（Aurora）？ 道德胚芽。
道德觀念也會很快聚集在黎明（Dawn）這類名號周圍。如果在夜間犯下了某樁罪行—我們所謂的「黑暗罪行」—誰能去發現或為其復仇呢？又是黎明，在她眾多名字之一的化身中—梵文的 *Ushas*、希臘文的 *Erinys*。
你或許會說，但為什麼人們在讚美厄俄斯（Eos）、感謝她之前，不曾質問她究竟是誰呢？這是一個難以回答的問題。然而，我們是否總是追問一個名字的真實含義、隱藏在名字背後的東西、名字的真實本質呢？我們談論天使，卻不曾問及他們由何構成。古人亦是如此，當他們稱烏莎斯（Ushas）為特尤斯或宙斯（Zeus）之女時，

心靈似乎得到了滿足，至少在一段時間內是如此。名稱具有驚人的滿足力量，只有少數人敢於掀開語言在自然上所覆蓋的面紗。當他們這樣做時，他們發現了什麼呢？他們發現無限者隱藏在一個名稱之下，而他們發現他們對無限者所能知曉的一切，就是這些名稱所表達的意義。烏莎（Ushas），晨曦之光，作為無限者的名稱，與特尤斯（Dyaus），蒼穹之神同樣恰當，後者後來演變成宙斯與朱庇特，只是它的歷史發展走向了不同的方向。請記住，我們自己雖然可能不再使用晨曦之光這個名稱來指稱無限者、彼岸、神聖者，但當我們談論上帝在自然界或我們心靈中的顯現時，我們仍然找不到比「光」更好的表達方式。
阿哈那（Ahana）、雅典娜（Athene）。
比較神話學的途徑到此為止還是平坦寬闊的。但語源學若能受到適當的制約，就能引領我們越過更崎嶇的道路，並在更幽暗的地帶為我們照亮光芒。
我之前說過，雖然烏莎斯（Ushas）是曙光最古老的名稱，其確立時間早於雅利安人分離之前，但同一現象從不同觀點來看，卻有許多其他名稱被賦予其上。這些名稱中，有些可能只被某位詩人使用，其他的則可能在某個家族或氏族中成為傳統；這些方言性的名稱由於其原初意義本身的不確定性，最容易被用於神話學的用語中。曙光作為烏莎斯已經成為神話化的，但正如我們在吠陀以及荷馬作品中所見，它的自然性格從未被完全遺忘。
現在在《吠陀》中還有另一個名稱用來指稱**神話意義上的**晨曦。435 那就是 Ahana。它在一首獻給烏莎絲（Ushas）的讚歌中僅出現過一次，而毫無疑問地，它乃是對晨曦的眾多美稱之一。

I. 123. 4. Gṛham-gṛham ahana yati dukha. Dive-dive adhi nama dadhīna, Śiśasanti dyotana śaśvat a agat Agram-agram it bhagate vasunam. 「阿哈那（Ahana）走向每一座房屋，為每一日賦予名字；迪婭塔那（Dyotana）總是急切地歸來尋求利得。她獲得了一切寶藏中最殊勝者。」

當我們詢問為何 Ahana 應該意指曙光時，答案是顯而易見的。Ahan 和 ahar 意指白晝，ahani 的雙數形式意指晝夜。在梵文神話中，Ahana 這個名字並未產生成果，但在希臘文中，如我們將看到的，它已成為一場宏大成長的萌芽。當我們詢問 Ahana 在何種形式下可能出現於希臘文時，我們應首先說為 'Axana 或 'Axna。這兩種形式都不存在。但我們必須記住，梵文的 h 代表三個原始音素，即 gh、dh 和 bh。例如，它在 dah（燃燒）中代表 gh，在其旁我們有梵文 ni-dagha（熱）。它在 NAH（聚合；nectere）中代表 dh，在其旁我們有 *NADH，其現在式為 naddha。它在同一個詞根 NAH 中代表 bh，在其旁我們有 NABH（在 nabhi 中）、GRAH 和 GRABH（兩者都意指抓取；to grab）。
在古希臘語本身中，我們發現送氣音方言學上產生了變化。我們不僅有 ὄρνις、ὀρνίθος，也有 ὄρνις、ὀρνιχός。我們有 ἵππα 和 ἱππία，以及類似的形式。
參見梵文 dahra = dab bra。

我們因此完全有理由期待見到 Athand 或 Aphand，而不是 Achand¹。現在 'κδ́ανα 在希臘文中作為雅典娜（Athene）的古老名稱而存在。我們也有 'Ἀδάναι 和 'Ἀθάνακα。在雅典娜中，我們看到與塞勒涅（Selene）相同的詞綴，而這兩個詞綴 ana 和 ana 之間的轉變已被證明極為常見²。因此在語音上，吠陀中的 Ahana 與希臘文中的 Athene 的辨識超越了批評和爭議的範圍。如果在此之後我們在神話上也將 Ahana 與 Athene 相辨識，我們必須清楚地看到我們的意思是什麼。首先，我們不能意指曾經存在過一個真實的存在體——一位婦女或女神——她在印度和希臘都為人所知，並在兩地皆被賦予相同的名稱 Ahana 和 Athand。
其次，我們不能認為在希臘文中關於雅典娜（Athene）所述說的一切，在梵文中也同樣被述說於阿哈那（Ahana）。
第三，最不可能的是，我們無法認為阿含那（Ahana）的祭祀是從印度傳入希臘，或雅典娜的祭祀是從希臘傳入印度的。
我們所能表達的只是，阿哈那（Ahana）作為曙光的名稱，在希臘語和梵語分化之前就已為人所知，而當這個神話萌芽在印度萎縮時，它卻在希臘發展成了光輝燦爛的成長。
我們在常用詞彙中看到同樣的現象。例如梵文中的 *bha*（भ），意為「分配」，而吠陀諸神之一的婆伽（Bhaga）原本意為「分配者與施恩者」。在琿德語中，*baga* 也以同樣的特徵出現，而在斯拉夫語族中，古斯拉夫語的 *bogii* 已成為「神」的通用名稱。在希臘語中，同一語根 φάγ 則完全喪失了¹ ¹ 《語言科學講座》，第二卷，第 34 頁。
2 庫恩，《火降之說》，第28頁。
神話學。437 分割的意義，並已進入一個新的途徑。它意味著進食，無論是在用我們的牙齒分割肉類的意義上（fyayovrts，牙齒，海西基烏斯〔Hesych.〕），還是在與他人分享一餐的意義上（如在賽易斯〔Sais〕，haivvfiL，bcurpos 等）。
所有這些必須充分承認，然而，正如我們無法解釋為什麼希臘語 pay 意為「吃」而不參照梵文 bhaj（分開）一樣，如果我們不知道梵文 Ahana 及其「曙光」之義，我們就無法理解為什麼偉大的希臘女神應該被稱為雅典娜（Athene）。

希臘學者經常主張，希臘人本身並不知道雅典娜（Athene）原本意為曙光，或者動詞 Qaydv 原本意為分割。這無疑是真的，同樣真確的是，只有少數希臘人知道宙斯（Zeus）原本意為蒼天，西風神（Zephyros）原本意為從太陽西沉之處吹來的風，或者北風神（Boreas）原本意為從北方群山吹來的風。我們不知道「主君」（Lord）原本意為「麵包賜與者」，或「公爵」（Duke）原本意為「領導與光明之人」；但惟有在知道了這一點之後，我們才能理解「主君」與「公爵」這兩個詞後來所獲得的歷史意涵的發展。
在雅典娜的神話故事中，也不是不可能發現某些蹤跡，指向她作為曙光女神的原始性格。她從宙斯的頭部誕生，如同吠陀中曙光從特尤斯的頭部升起（murdha DivaA）；同樣的意義下，她被稱為「Koryphasia」（來自 Kopvcf)），並且她在義大利的對應神祇被稱為「Cap(i)ta」。她的純潔指向曙光的純潔，她的智慧
自由比作晨曦光芒的燦爛，她的勇武比作她光線的勢不可擋。她性格中的其他一切都可稱為希臘式的，無法藉由對吠陀思想的任何參考而解釋。但對於神話學研究者最為有趣的，即女神的萌芽理念，除了在阿赫那（Ahana）的名字中外，無處可尋；若非《梨俱吠陀》中我向各位引述的那唯一詩句，這個名字在印度也早已被遺忘。
達芙涅。
我相信到此為止，我們都在堅實的基礎上。但我認為我們可以再進一步。我們看到梵文中表示早晨或白晝的詞是 *ahan* 或 *ahar*，其原始含義是光亮。現在日耳曼語族中表示白晝的詞源自詞根 *dah*，意為燃燒、炎熱。哥德語 *dags*、盎格魯撒克遜語 *doeg*、英語 *day*，都預設了詞根 **DHAGH**，而這個詞根在梵文中確實存在，為 *dah*，意為燃燒。

兩個詞根 AH（由此衍生 ah、an、day）和 DAH（由此衍生哥德語 dags、day）是否為平行詞根，這個問題只能透過對一般原則的充分討論才能決定。說初始的 d 在 dah 中遺失，這是毫無根據的，因為初始的 d 若非有充分理由，從不會遺失。同樣的論證也適用於相反的理論，即初始的 d 被添加到詞根 AH。我們所能說的是，還有其他情況見到平行詞根，一個具有初始的 d，另一個則無。這是否純粹出於偶然，我們目前無法斷定；我們所能說的是，確實存在著支持這一過程的類比。例如，我們在梵文中發現 *aru（淚），大概衍生自

神話學。439 一個字根 AS，意為尖銳而刻薄；而我們在希臘文 δάκρυ（淚）中發現，其明顯是衍生自字根 DAḲ，意為咬嚙¹。我們是否應該相信這兩個字詞毫無共同之處，且它們的起源不欠於一個共同的隱喻觀念，即尖銳與咬嚙的觀念，因而也不欠於一個共同的創造行為？兩個字根 AS 與 DAḲ 都存在於不同的雅利安語族中，並已證明其具有生產力。從 AS（尖銳之意，在每個意義上）出發，我們在梵文中有 asra 與 asri（尖端、邊緣），在拉丁文中有 acus（鹿），在希臘文中有 ἄκμη 與 ἄκρις。正如 acidus 從意為尖銳而逐漸轉為意為苦澀與酸味，梵文的 asru 與斯拉夫語的 aszara、立陶宛語的 aszara 也逐漸轉為意為苦澀的淚。

從梵文 DAaS'（咬，苦來自於咬的動作，梵文 bhid，拉丁文 /i ado）出發，我們在希臘文中有 b&Kpv，在拉丁文中有 lacruma，在哥德文中有 tagr，在英文中有 tear，誰能懷疑這些詞語原本都是指「刺痛的淚水」呢？當然，我們可以對任何事物表示懷疑，而由於懷疑往往看起來比接受更加學問淵博，搖頭否定的誘惑就非常強大。然而正是由於這個原因，這種廉價的懷疑論應當受到尖銳的批評，例如波特教授最近就向一位學識淵博的同事發出過這樣的批評。他寫道：「當然，教授明確反對的語氣——當他說『asru 與 dasru 的比較並不比 ahan 與 day 的比較更加有根據』時——這實際上毫無意義2。」
但即使是那些堅持詞根 1 Kick 的含義可以延伸得更遠的學者們，比如將梵文 agni（火）追溯到詞根 DAH；荷茲曼指出《摩訶婆羅多》中的女神 Danayु 在《哈里瓦薩》中作為 Anavu 出現；見荷茲曼著《阿耆尼》，第 34 頁。
波特《語源學研究》（Etymologische Forschungen）第二卷第一冊，第510頁。
AH 與詞根 DAH 毫無關聯，但我們不能否認 Dahana 可以成為詞根 DAH 的完全合理的衍生詞，而這個詞根曾在日耳曼語族中給予我們「日」(day) 的名稱。該詞根 DAH 預設了一個詞根 DHAGH，並隸屬於根據葛拉斯曼（Grassmann）觀察所發現的整個詞根類別，在該類別中，初始和最終的送氣音必然在哥德語中由初始和最終的濁音來表現。

由於詞根 DAH 中最後的 h 可能代表原始的 gh 或 bh，我們得到兩種可能的變體，DAGH 和 DABH。DAGH 在梵文 *idagha*（熱）中存在；DABH 在希臘文中會出現為 *daphnē*。從這個 *daphnē*，希臘人通過極其規則的過程可以推導出 *daphnē*，而該名詞的含義將與梵文中的 *Ahana* 相同，亦即燃燒者、光明的、燦爛的。在 *Aaphnē* 一側，我們有色薩利亞方言形式 *Daphnē*，帶有 DAH 的喉音尾音，而赫西基烏斯提到 *daphnion* 作為月桂樹木材的名稱，因為它易於燃燒（*eukauston*）。
若我們已知道斐波亞（Phoeboa）意指太陽——少有學者會否認這一點——且達芙妮（Daphne）可能意指曙光，那麼我們大概無須費力尋求太遠，就能解釋這則希臘傳說：曙光在太陽面前逃奔，當他欲擁抱她時，她便消隱而去。
但為何──人們可能會問──黛芙妮（Daphne）據稱被變成了月桂樹呢？族群心理學的神話學家將告訴我們，在薩摩亞、沙勞越及其他未開化的國家中，男人和女人 ¹ M. M., *Comparative Mythology*, 1856; *Selected Essays*, i. p. 398.

在 441 頁稱某些事物能夠轉變成植物，並聲稱既然希臘人曾經是野蠻人，他們無疑也相信同樣的事物，因此我們無須進一步探究。現在，儘管充分尊重民族心理學家——有時他們被稱為民俗學家——我不認為這不過是以未知解釋未知而已。每個人都會提出的問題是，那麼薩摩亞人、砂拉越人及其他野蠻民族為何相信男人和女人會變成樹木呢？圖騰崇拜當然不能、拜物教也不能、禁忌制度也不能說明他們為何會有這種信念。那麼，為什麼不允許古典學者在更近的地方尋找鑰匙呢？當他發現月桂樹因為容易燃燒的木材而被稱為 δάφνη（火木），為什麼不允許他說達芙妮傳說——晨曦變成月桂樹——可能是源於語言對思想的影響，源於語言某種自我遺忘的現象——實際上源於同一種影響，這種影響曾促使人們採納涉水過河的牛作為牛津的紋章呢？
「何必遠尋？看吧，善就在眼前！」

無論同名現象（如 Daphne 與 daphne）是否同樣構成薩摩亞人及沙勞越人關於男女可變為植物之信仰的根底，這是一個遠為困難的問題；在我們就此類事務進行概括之前，詳細探究若干個別案例要好得多，例如許阿（Hya-）等案例¹。 --- ¹ 參見《語言科學講演集》第二卷，54b 頁；《精選論文集》第一卷，309 頁；δάφνον ἐγκαινότον ξύλον δωρήτης（Hesych. 上引）。
